Pearson Edexcel International GCSE
Mark Scheme
Summer 2026 · Predicted Paper (Hard)
In Economics (4EC1)
Paper 2: Macroeconomics and the Global Economy


General Marking Guidance
   •   All candidates must receive the same treatment. Examiners must mark the first candidate in
       exactly the same way as they mark the last.
   •   Mark schemes should be applied positively. Candidates must be rewarded for what they
       have shown they can do rather than penalised for omissions.
   •   Examiners should mark according to the mark scheme not according to their perception of
       where the grade boundaries may lie.
   •   There is no ceiling on achievement. All marks on the mark scheme should be used
       appropriately.
   •   All the marks on the mark scheme are designed to be awarded. Examiners should always
       award full marks if deserved.
   •   Where some judgement is required, mark schemes will provide the principles by which
       marks will be awarded and exemplification may be limited.
   •   When examiners are in doubt regarding the application of the mark scheme to a candidate's
       response, the team leader must be consulted.
   •   Crossed out work should be marked UNLESS the candidate has replaced it with an
       alternative response.
Mark Scheme: Paper 2 · Macroeconomics and the Global Economy (4EC1/02) · Predicted
(Hard)

 1(a)      Question                                                              (1)
           Which one of the following is a possible disadvantage of MNCs for a
           host country?
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                     AO1 1 mark
           The only correct answer is:
           C Moving profits back to the home country

           A is not correct because technology transfer is an advantage of
           MNCs.
           B is not correct because job creation is an advantage of MNCs.
           D is not correct because paying taxes is a benefit to the host
           government.


 1(b)      Question                                                              (1)
           Which one of the following is measured by the Human Development
           Index (HDI)?
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                     AO1 1 mark
           The only correct answer is:
           A GNI per capita, education and life expectancy

           B is not correct because these are macroeconomic indicators, not
           HDI components.
           C is not correct because these are balance of payments
           components.
           D is not correct because HDI does not include these as indicators.


 1(c)      Question                                                              (2)
           What is meant by the term balance of trade?
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                    AO1 2 marks
           Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition:
           The difference between the value of a country's exports and imports
           (1) of goods and services (1)

           OR

           Exports minus imports (1) over a given period of time (1)

           Accept any other appropriate response


 1(d)      Question                                                              (2)
           Describe one way an MNC can create jobs in a host country.
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                   AO1 2 marks
       Award 1 mark for identification and 1 mark for development.

       One way is by building a new factory or office in the host country (1)
       which directly employs local workers in production, administration
       and management (1)

       OR

       One way is through indirect employment in the supply chain (1) as
       local firms provide goods and services to the MNC, such as
       transport, catering and raw materials (1)

       Accept any other appropriate response


1(e)   Question                                                                 (2)
       Calculate Nigeria's nominal GDP per capita in US$ in 2023.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 2 marks
       Award 1 mark for correct method (GDP / Population):
       US$477bn ÷ 220m (1)

       Award 1 mark for the correct answer:
       = US$2,168.18 (accept US$2,168 or US$2,170)

       Award 2 marks for any of these correct answers even without
       working.
       Award 1 mark if US$ is missing (e.g. just 2,168).
       Do not award marks for the formula alone.


1(f)   Question                                                                 (3)
       Draw the effects of removing the price ceiling on petrol.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       The ceiling was set below the equilibrium price, so removal returns
       the market to free-market equilibrium.

       Award 1 mark for removal of the horizontal price ceiling line OR
       showing a new higher equilibrium at the intersection of S and D.
       Award 1 mark for a higher new equilibrium price, labelled (e.g. P1 or
       Pe).
       Award 1 mark for a higher new equilibrium quantity (compared to the
       quantity supplied under the ceiling), labelled (e.g. Q1 or Qe).


1(g)   Question                                                                 (3)
       Explain one economic cost of high youth unemployment for Nigeria.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                       AO2 3 marks
          Award 1 mark for identifying a relevant cost.
          Award 1 mark for development.
          Award 1 mark for context (Nigeria).

          One cost is lost output / reduced GDP (1). With 40% of 15–34 year
          olds unemployed, a significant proportion of Nigeria's potential labour
          force is not producing goods and services (1), meaning the economy
          operates below its production possibility frontier and loses billions in
          national output (1).

          Accept any other appropriate response · e.g. higher welfare
          spending, lower tax revenue, social unrest, loss of human capital,
          increased brain drain.


1(h)      Question                                                                       (6)
          Analyse the possible impact of FDI on the Nigerian economy.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)

          AO2 and AO3 · accept positive or negative impacts
            • FDI is when a foreign firm invests directly in productive
                assets in another country, such as building factories or
                acquiring firms.
              •   US$4.5bn of FDI brings capital inflows into Nigeria that can
                  finance infrastructure and industrial development.
              •   FDI creates direct and indirect jobs, which is critical given
                  Nigeria's 33.3% unemployment rate.
              •   Technology and knowledge transfer from foreign MNCs
                  improves productivity and skills of Nigerian workers.
              •   Higher employment and wages feed through to higher
                  consumer spending, stimulating aggregate demand and
                  growth.
              •   FDI often leads to higher tax revenues for the Nigerian
                  Government to fund public services.
              •   However, profits may be repatriated to the home country,
                  reducing long-term benefits to Nigeria.
              •   Some FDI in oil and gas can cause environmental damage
                  and overdependence on primary products.


Level     Mark         Descriptor

          0                •   No rewardable material.

Level 1   1–2              •   Demonstrates basic knowledge and understanding by
                               developing relevant points. Limited application of economic
                               terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                               Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a
                               lack of analysis of issues (AO3).
Level     Mark   Descriptor

Level 2   3–4       •   Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by
                        developing relevant points. Partial application of economic
                        terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                    •   Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                        organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be
                        good, with some analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 3   5–6       •   Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by
                        developing relevant points. Appropriate application of
                        economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                    •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
                        and organisation. Interpretation of economic information will
                        be excellent, with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
2(a)   Question                                                               (1)
       Which one of the following is an example of an indirect tax?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO1 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       C Value Added Tax (VAT) on goods

       A is not correct because corporation tax is a direct tax on profits.
       B is not correct because income tax is a direct tax on wages.
       D is not correct because inheritance tax is a direct tax on estates.


2(b)   Question                                                               (1)
       Which one of the following is the best description of frictional
       unemployment?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO1 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       B Unemployment between jobs while searching for a new role

       A is not correct because this describes cyclical unemployment.
       C is not correct because this describes structural unemployment.
       D is not correct because this describes seasonal unemployment.


2(c)   Question                                                               (1)
       State one cause of a government budget deficit.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO1 1 mark
       Award 1 mark for any one cause:
         • Higher government spending
            •   Lower tax revenues (e.g. during a recession)
            •   Cutting tax rates
            •   Increased welfare payments during a downturn
            •   Higher interest payments on national debt
            •   Economic shocks (e.g. pandemic, financial crisis)


       Accept any other appropriate response


2(d)   Question                                                               (2)
       What is meant by the term regressive tax?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO1 2 marks
       Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition:
       A tax that takes a larger percentage from those on lower incomes (1)
       compared to those on higher incomes (1)

       OR
       A tax where the tax rate falls (1) as income rises (1)

       Accept reference to examples (VAT, excise duties) but not for the
       mark itself


2(e)   Question                                                               (3)
       Explain one way a VAT increase will affect UK consumers.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       Award 1 mark for identification of a way.
       Award 1 mark for development.
       Award 1 mark for context (UK / 2% VAT rise).

       One way is that higher VAT raises the prices of affected goods (1).
       Producers pass some of the 2% rise in VAT on to consumers (1),
       which reduces UK consumers' real income and purchasing power,
       particularly affecting low-income households (1).

       Accept any other appropriate response.


2(f)   Question                                                               (3)
       Label the remaining three stages of the economic cycle.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       Award 1 mark for Boom (top of first curve).
       Award 1 mark for Downturn (between Boom and Recession).
       Award 1 mark for Recovery (rising portion after Recession).


2(g)   Question                                                               (9)
       Assess the possible benefits for the UK of increasing excise duty on
       cigarettes.
       Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3
       marks)

       AO2 and AO3 · benefits
         • Excise duty is a specific tax on certain goods; increasing it
             raises the price of cigarettes.
          •   Higher prices reduce demand for cigarettes, particularly from
              younger and price-sensitive smokers, reducing smoking rates
              below 13%.
          •   Lower smoking rates reduce negative externalities of
              consumption · second-hand smoke, NHS treatment costs.
          •   Cigarettes have price-inelastic demand in the short run, so
              the government can raise significant tax revenue from
              existing smokers.
          •   Revenue can be used to fund healthcare (NHS), education or
                  anti-smoking campaigns.
              •   Improved public health leads to lower absenteeism and
                  higher productivity in the UK economy.


          AO4 · drawbacks / evaluation
            • Excise duty is a regressive tax · it takes a larger share of
               income from lower-income smokers, worsening inequality.
              •   Higher prices may encourage black market / smuggled
                  cigarettes, reducing government revenue and creating
                  enforcement costs.
              •   If demand is price-inelastic, few smokers will quit, so health
                  benefits will be limited.
              •   Tobacco farmers and shops lose revenue; related industries
                  may see job losses.
              •   Effectiveness depends on the price elasticity of demand ·
                  typically quite low in the short run but higher in the long run.
              •   Overall judgement: the benefits are real but partly offset by
                  the regressive nature and black-market risk; complementary
                  measures such as anti-smoking campaigns may be more
                  effective.


Level     Mark         Descriptor

          0                •   No rewardable material.

Level 1   1–3              •   Demonstrates basic knowledge and understanding by
                               developing relevant points. Limited application of economic
                               terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                               Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a
                               lack of analysis of issues (AO3).
                           •   Only offers one viewpoint, meaning there is an unbalanced
                               and incomplete evaluation, showing limited understanding
                               and awareness (AO4).

Level 2   4–6              •   Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by
                               developing relevant points. Partial application of economic
                               terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                               organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be
                               good, with some analysis of issues (AO3).
                           •   Offers more than one viewpoint but the argument may lack
                               balance, leading to an evaluation that may not demonstrate
                               full understanding and awareness (AO4).

Level 3   7–9              •   Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by
                               developing relevant points. Appropriate application of
                               economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
                               and organisation. Interpretation of economic information will
                               be excellent, with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
Level   Mark   Descriptor

                  •   Offers more than one viewpoint. The argument is well
                      balanced and coherent, leading to an evaluation that
                      demonstrates full understanding and awareness (AO4).
3(a)   Question                                                                      (1)
       Which one of the following is an example of a tariff?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO2 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       C A tax placed on goods as they enter a country

       A is not correct because this describes an embargo.
       B is not correct because this describes a quota.
       D is not correct because this describes an export subsidy.


3(b)   Question                                                                      (1)
       Which one of the following is the most likely impact of the US dollar
       appreciation on US trade with China?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO2 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       C US exports become more expensive for Chinese buyers

       A is not correct because an appreciation makes US exports more
       expensive, not cheaper.
       B is not correct because an appreciation makes imports cheaper, not
       more expensive.
       D is not correct because China's trade surplus with the US is more
       likely to rise, not fall.


3(c)   Question                                                                      (3)
       Draw the effects of a 25% tariff on Chinese steel imports into the US.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       A tariff raises costs for importers, shifting the supply curve to the left.

       Award 1 mark for a leftward shift of the supply curve, labelled S1
       (tariff) or similar.
       Award 1 mark for a higher new equilibrium price, labelled (e.g. P1).
       Award 1 mark for a lower new equilibrium quantity, labelled (e.g.
       Q1).


3(d)   Question                                                                      (6)
       Analyse the possible advantages of tariffs for domestic producers in
       the US.
       Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)

       AO2 and AO3
         • A tariff is a tax on imports, raising the domestic price of
             foreign goods and making US-made steel more competitive.
           •   US steel producers can charge higher prices and/or sell more
               steel as consumers switch from now-expensive Chinese
                  imports.
              •   Higher revenue and profits allow US producers to invest in
                  new plants and technology.
              •   Protection shields 'infant industries' and allows US firms to
                  grow, achieve economies of scale, and compete
                  internationally.
              •   With $370bn of imports facing tariffs, the scale of protection
                  is significant and can secure long-term market share.
              •   Increased production creates jobs in steel, aluminium and
                  related supply chains · boosting local economies in steel-
                  producing regions.
              •   Tariffs may reduce US reliance on Chinese supply, improving
                  national security in strategic industries.

3(e)      Question                                                                         (9)
          Assess the possible impact of a currency depreciation on an
          economy such as China's.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3
          marks)

          AO2 and AO3 · benefits of depreciation
            • A depreciation means the yuan loses value against other
                currencies (here, 8% against the US$).
              •   Chinese exports become cheaper in foreign markets,
                  boosting demand for Chinese goods · helpful as China
                  exported $3.6 trillion in 2023.
              •   Rising exports contribute to higher GDP and economic
                  growth, and support employment in export industries.
              •   An improved trade balance can help fund development and
                  increase foreign exchange reserves.
              •   Tourism into China may rise as foreign visitors find China
                  cheaper to visit.


          AO4 · drawbacks / evaluation
            • Imports become more expensive, including oil, commodities
               and high-tech components · this can cause imported/cost-
               push inflation.
              •   Chinese firms reliant on imported inputs see higher costs,
                  reducing profits and potentially employment.
              •   Depreciation can trigger currency wars and retaliation from
                  trading partners (e.g. further US tariffs).
              •   The effect on the current account depends on the Marshall–
                  Lerner condition · whether PEDx + PEDm > 1.
              •   If the depreciation is seen as a deliberate policy, it may
                  worsen trade tensions with the US and EU.
              •   Overall judgement: short-term export gains are real, but long-
                  term effects depend on elasticities, inflation consequences
                  and trading-partner responses.

4(a)      Question                                                                      (2)
          Calculate Turkey's real interest rate in 2024.
          Answer / Indicative Content
                                   AO2 2 marks
          Award 1 mark for correct method (Nominal rate − inflation rate):
          45% − 67% (1)

          Award 1 mark for the correct answer:
          = −22% (1)

          Award 2 marks for the correct answer of −22% even without working.
          Award 1 mark if the % symbol or negative sign is missing.
          Do not award marks for the formula alone.


4(b)      Question                                                                      (6)
          Analyse the relationship between interest rates and the exchange
          rate for a country such as Turkey.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)

          AO2 and AO3
            • Interest rates are the cost of borrowing / return on saving; the
                exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of
                another.
              •   Higher interest rates increase the returns available to foreign
                  investors who deposit money in Turkish banks.
              •   This raises demand for the Turkish lira on foreign exchange
                  markets (hot money flows).
              •   Increased demand for lira causes the exchange rate to
                  appreciate against other currencies.
              •   A stronger lira makes Turkish imports cheaper, which helps
                  to reduce imported/cost-push inflation · particularly
                  important given 67% inflation.
              •   Conversely, cutting interest rates reduces returns to
                  investors, causing capital outflows and lira depreciation,
                  which raises inflation.
              •   With Turkey's high real rate (nominal 45%), foreign investors
                  may still be cautious if they expect continued inflation to
                  erode returns.

          0               •   No rewardable material.

                          •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.

                              lack of analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 3   5–6             •   Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by
                              developing relevant points. Appropriate application of
                              economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
                              and organisation. Interpretation of economic information will
                              be excellent, with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).


4(c)      Question                                                                   (12)
          Evaluate the effectiveness of using supply-side policies to control
          inflation in a country such as Turkey.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (4 marks) / AO3 (4 marks) / AO4 (4
          marks)

          AO2 and AO3 · advantages
            • Supply-side policies are measures designed to increase the
                productive capacity of the economy (e.g. education and
                training, infrastructure, labour-market reform).
             •   By increasing productive capacity, supply-side policies shift
                 long-run aggregate supply to the right, putting downward
                 pressure on prices.
             •   Investment in education and training raises productivity of
                 Turkish workers, enabling firms to produce more without
                 raising prices.
             •   Improved infrastructure reduces transport and logistics costs,
                 lowering costs of production for Turkish firms.
             •   Labour market reforms (e.g. reducing hiring restrictions) can
                 lower unemployment and reduce wage-push inflation.
             •   Unlike raising interest rates (which slows growth), supply-side
                 policies support growth while controlling inflation · a better
                 long-term solution for Turkey.
             •   Can address cost-push inflation directly, which may be a root
                 cause of Turkey's 67% inflation.


          AO4 · disadvantages / evaluation
            • Supply-side policies take many years to work · education
               reforms may take a decade to fully affect productivity · while
               Turkey faces inflation now.
             •   They are often expensive (e.g. infrastructure investment),
                 adding to government spending and possibly worsening the
                 budget deficit.
             •   Effectiveness depends on the type of inflation · if Turkey's
                 inflation is mainly demand-pull or imported, supply-side
                  policies alone will not solve it.
              •   Supply-side reforms (e.g. labour market flexibility) can be
                  politically unpopular and face resistance.
              •   With very high inflation (67%), immediate demand-side tools
                  (monetary policy, fiscal tightening) are also needed.
              •   Tax cuts, if used as a supply-side tool, may worsen Turkey's
                  fiscal position in the short term.
              •   Overall judgement: supply-side policies are an important
                  long-term tool to control inflation sustainably, but Turkey
                  needs a combination of supply-side and demand-side
                  policies (especially tighter monetary policy) to tackle very
                  high inflation in the short run. The effectiveness depends on
                  the speed of implementation, funding, and the root causes of
                  inflation.


Level     Mark         Descriptor

          0                •   No rewardable material.

Level 1   1–4              •   Demonstrates isolated elements of relevant knowledge and
                               understanding; may lack development of relevant points.
                               Limited application of economic terms, concepts, theories
                               and calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                               Chain of reasoning may be attempted. Interpretation of
                               economic information will be limited, with a lack of analysis of
                               issues (AO3).
                           •   Only offers one viewpoint, meaning there is an unbalanced
                               and incomplete evaluation, showing limited understanding
                               and awareness. A judgement or conclusion is unlikely to be
                               attempted (AO4).

Level 2   5–8              •   Demonstrates partial selectivity and some accurate
                               knowledge and understanding by developing some relevant
                               points. Partial application of economic terms, concepts,
                               theories and calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                               organisation. Chain of reasoning will be present but may be
                               limited. Interpretation of economic information will be good,
                               with some analysis of issues (AO3).
                           •   Offers more than one viewpoint but the argument may lack
                               balance, leading to an evaluation that may not demonstrate
                               full understanding and awareness. A judgement or
                               conclusion is attempted, but may not be fully supported
                               (AO4).

Level 3   9–12             •   Demonstrates specific and accurate knowledge and
                               understanding by developing relevant points. Appropriate
                               application of economic terms, concepts, theories and
                               calculations (AO2).
                           •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
                               and organisation. Chain of reasoning will be coherent and
                               logical. Interpretation of economic information will be
Level   Mark   Descriptor

                      excellent with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
                  •   Offers more than one viewpoint. The argument is well
                      balanced and coherent, leading to an evaluation that
                      demonstrates full understanding and awareness. A supported
                      judgement or conclusion is present (AO4).
